speaker: interviewer
text: --Traub's interview-- you were telling us about high school and you had mentioned this was a time when rumors at dinner parties were starting to trickle in, if you could go back to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes but always, it was happening somewhere else. And this was not yet war. It was in the '30s when Hitler took over. And then, I remember very clearly, for instance, the Evian Conference. At the time-- that happened in 1936. Now at the time, I was only 11 years old and clearly not involved in politics, but rather involved in my studies and being quote, 'a nice girl,' playing the piano, and-- and-- and going to my French teacher, and so forth. But I do remember my elders talking about the Evian Conference, that Roosevelt-- I mean, he was considered the messiah, or the saint, or the savior-- is going to put an end to this madman. And there was this Evian Conference. That's all I remember. Next thing I remember was when-- I believe it was the Munich Conference when Daladier, Chamberlain, Mussolini, and Hitler met. And I believe it was in Munich. I believe in 1938-- I am not sure about the date-- but this is when it began, the chopping up of the various countries. Namely, they gave Sudetenland to Germany. And so I remember a magazine, the front page, a four clover leaf. And in each section of the four-- of-- of-- of the leaf of the clover, there was one Daladier, one Chamberlain, one Hitler, and one Mussolini. Peace-- it's going to be peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your experience of all this news, your personal experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: My personal experience was none because my life was not affected. I still had my piano lessons. I still went for my French lessons, which were aside of the school curriculum. The Shabbats were still maintained. The business was going on. My brothers eventually got married. But this was somewhere else, and there was Roosevelt and the West. I mean, OK, so Hitler got this chunk of-- of-- of Czechoslovakia, Sudetenland, so that's going to keep him happy and peace. You know, I mean, human beings are human beings. I mean, this was the, the almost pathetic naivete of the people in my town, or to be more specific, my family, my father not reading the script correctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the script he was reading?

speaker: interviewee
text: The script he was reading was probably denial of reality. Although in those days, he wasn't alone in denying reality because Chamberlain, the premier of-- of England, went back with a piece of paper. And-- and he said, 'Peace in our time,' famous last words.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was not the only one. This was the general belief, that, you know, this madness cannot last, particularly when there were-- the news came that in Germany butter was ersatz butter and ersatz coffee. And, I mean, a civilized people will not stand for this kind of a mad dictator. By this time, Hitler wasn't only a madman. He was also referred to more or less as 'the mad dictator.' How and when did it start changing for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it started to change when the Hungarians occupied my town, or 2/5 of Transylvania. And that was already in 1940. Second World War was declared in 1939. And the concept of 'blitzkrieg' or the, the-- not the concept, but rather the word, the idea of 'blitzkrieg' was introduced when Hitler occupied Poland in a matter of weeks. And then war broke out. I mean, he declared war on France on May 1940. And on August 1940, there was a conference in Vienna. It was called, I believe, the Second Award Conference, in which, in which Hungary, Romania were part of. And Hitler awarded 2/5 of Transylvania to Hungary, who became Hitler's ally, and gave nothing to Romania as punishment for Romania not joining the Axis. And then, Sighet became Hungary. And I think the tragedy of the not only Hungarian Jews but the tragedy of the Transylvanian Jews began.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you walk us through it for you step-by-step?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father, who grew up during the Austro-Hungarian regime, was always at heart a Hungarian patriot. I remember him telling me that when he was in school, every-- for several years while he was in high school came March 15, which was one of the great Hungarian holidays, something like 4th of July. He was the one who would recite a very famous poem by the great national poet Petofi, Andrej Petofi, Andre. The-- the poem was [HUNGARIAN]. I mean, I didn't learn the poem, but I remember my father telling me. Hungarians, stand up. Get up. Join. The homeland is calling you and, you know, exhorting the person to be patriotic and stand up for what's right and for the Hungarian homeland. And so my father was truly a Hungarian patriot. And when the occupation occurred and the day before, the Hungarian contingent army was supposed to occupy Sighet, which was early September.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The night before-- 1940. The night before-- because the award was done in August, end of August. So it took a little time to, to, to get the logistics about occupation. The Romanians had to withdraw, and then the Hungarians came into my town. The night before and the day before, my father bedecked the town, the main street, with silk flags that he had little seamstresses sew throughout the night. He gave the silk, red, green, and white. That was not necessarily the sequence of the way that-- I-- I forgot and I don't even want to remember, perhaps, anymore-- but these were the three colors. And those flags were my father's flags. And I remember standing with him as we were watching the Hungarian cavalry, the Hussars, come in and the gendarmes with the plume helmet, the gendarmes. And my father-- I looked up at him. He was a very tall man, a very handsome man and I adored him, clearly. I looked up at him, and I saw tears streaming down his cheek 'because these are my people,' he said finally.

speaker: interviewer
text: He never spoke Romanian, by the way, you know, until then, although it was Romania. My mother spoke Romanian but not well. Of course, I spoke fluently. And many years later, I remember telling my father-- and I wish I wouldn't have told him, perhaps. I remember telling him, Daddy, remember how you wept? Your Hungarians, look what they are doing. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first year, 1941, 1942, Hungarians didn't do much as far as my life-- as far as our life was concerned. You see, the town-- you asked me earlier and I really didn't elaborate. The town was sort of stratified, even within the Jewish communities. You know, there were the well-to-do, the, you know, upper, middle, lower. The poorest section was living in another side of the town. We were living in the not poor section. And so that life continued for a long time, except there were little things, little things. I think before they got to the Jews, the Hungarians got to the Romanians, where there has always been and still is probably, a tremendous ethnic-- not conflict-- hatred between them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first in school-- and these are my memories-- first, they forbade-- the Hungarians forbade any one of us to speak among ourselves because the mother tongue was Hungarian. They forbade to ever use a Romanian word. Because if anybody was caught using a Romanian word, you had to pay a fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the way, it was the same thing during the Romanian schooling. I remember that very well. And there, it was even a little more difficult because most of us spoke Hungarian. That was our first language. But anybody caught speaking had to pay a fine, one leu, or during the Hungarians, one pengo. So that was the first-- they-- they were after the Romanians. Then, the Romanians, most of the functionaries left town. The Jews were more or less all right except in 1942, the Hungarian government issued a law which said all those Jews who do not have Hungarian citizenship-- in other words, are not native Hungarians-- or born in the city of Sighet, all those Jews who are foreigners from Poland, specifically, have to report because they are going to be resettled in Poland. Now, that was a terrible thing because there were lots of Jews who were not native Hungarians or Romanians, depending on who were born there. And in fact, my brother Alex, Shandor, had a girlfriend at that time. I remember her very well, a beautiful girl , [NON-ENGLISH]. And her parents were from Poland, who apparently never bothered to get the Hungarian citizenship in 1940. Or maybe they-- I really don't know. But they were-- all those Jews who were considered not 'Hungarians' were taken and deported. Now, we were told that they were just resettled in Poland to their country of origin, except that some rumors began to seep through that this transport was murdered somewhere on the way. I think, As I look back now, I think that was the first harbinger of things to come. But I tell you, nobody really registered. Nobody registered because it was too horrendous to contemplate that. This was in 1942. Soon thereafter, certain laws were put in effect and this is where I personally was touched. Children, Jewish children could not attend high school-- and that was called [GERMAN], unless their parents, their father, had some sort of a decoration during the first war, First World War, as being part of the Austro-Hungarian regime and being in the army. Well, my father was not in the army, but my uncle was, my uncle who forbade contraband. And he, in fact, got some sort of a gold decoration from the Hungarian regime during the First World War because of some act of heroism. He saved, et cetera. And he went to try to intercede that I should be permitted to go to school because I was a good student, et cetera. No way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was out, but some Jewish girls could still go because their fathers did have that decoration. What happened to me then was that, at that point, the Hungarian government permitted two-- accredited-- two Jewish high schools, one in Budapest and one in Kolozsvar. But again, it was very difficult to get in because, as you can imagine, every Jewish child whose father was not decorated by the Hungarians during the First World War wanted to go to school. So there was a very heavy selection process. And I did get in. And I think, I think it was the best education I ever got because what also happened after numerous clauses was introduced, they also fired all the Jewish professors who taught at-- in Hungarian universities. So we had the best of the best among the educators teaching us, teaching high school students. So I was sent to Kolozsvar for my studies. How was that for you to be so involved in your first high school and suddenly have to stop and change and leave home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, it was terrible, but it was also an adventure because it was the first time that I was going away from home. And I felt sort of rather confident because I was an A student all around. And although I heard that this is a very special school and you really have to be very, very good, I figured, well, I was a first-- you know, I was-- I always got prizes and so I'll have no problem. I didn't really have a problem when I went to school, except that everybody else there was an A student, you see, because they were really-- they-- they had to limit the number of the students they were taking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to leave your friends in the other high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was not good. It was very hard. The thing that made it easier for me was that one of my friends-- her name is Susanna Glazinger, I remember-- she also was accepted. So the two of us-- my mother came with us. And she took-- she made sure that we are going to stay with somebody very appropriate. And that somebody very appropriate was nobody less than the chairman of the board of the Jewish school. So the girls were in good hands. Nothing, God forbid, could happen to them, which we were very nicely treated. And although I remember I was very lonesome for my mother, for my mother very much so, and for my brothers, and all the goods things. But somehow, there was such a wonderful intellectual ferment-- as I look back, I call it an 'intellectual ferment'-- in that school because we had such terrific teachers, that I became caught up with that And I stayed that way till March 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 1944 was the day that the Hungarian government, Horthy, the regent, and his government asked Adolf Eichmann to come into Hungary and take-- and take care-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] handle its Hungarian Jewish population. And that is when everything turned to ashes, in a way. Eichmann came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Kolozsvar, my last year just before going for the baccalaureate, which was supposed to take place in July, June, July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what month are we in now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are in March. And it was a terrible time for me and for my friend Susie because we were here and our parents were there. Kolozsvar is in southern Transylvania. Sighet is in northern Transylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 400 miles, but in those days, the trains were not-- the tests were not very fast trains. And it could have been a problem. And the problem was also, do we leave now or are we going to wait until we have our baccalaureate? Well, what happened-- and we called the families and the telephoning went back and forth. Stay. Graduate. No, come home. What happened was that the school itself changed the date. And, you know, I mean, I think I had the easiest baccalaureate ever because the Nazis occupied the lower part of the school, and we were having our baccalaureate on the upper part of the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can tell you about the day. I can tell you the day or the week prior to the baccalaureate because, when the Nazis marched in, as I said, I was in school. And somebody heard it on the radio, and somebody came with the news, apparently, I think, that they are here. They are here. When I say 'the Nazis,' it was a very small contingent. They were-- I don't even know. I-- I remember just seeing one SS officer, who came to the school, and at this point, we all ran to the windows and looked down in the courtyard. And I remember seeing him do this and barking some orders- couldn't hear. Clearly, he asked for the principal. And I remember the principal coming out. I remember him wearing glasses. And the Nazi was telling him whatever he was telling him. It was a terrible-- it was very frightening that one man can do all this. And so we were told-- the children were immediately dismissed, sent home, but that they will permit the baccalaureate exam if it can be done within a week. And that is when I called my parents. And they said, let's take the chance. You should do that. And so the Nazis were billeted downstairs. And we had about two classes-- two rooms in which the exams were taken. And it was the easiest, I mean, because the baccalaureate was supposed to be a very, very serious exam. It was the equivalent of two years college at that point because the educational system was different than it is here. It wasn't high school. You had eight years of Gymnasium. And after that, you went to university, which was, you know, the second-- as I said, equivalent of two years college by that time. But it was very easy, I must say. And we took a deep breath, Susie and I. And we are going to go back to Sighet. And we got into the last train, I believe, pulling out from Kolozsvar to Sighet. And it kept stopping quite often because other trains were coming. And I remember those other trains for which this train had to stop were coming from the east and were full of wounded soldiers, Wehrmacht. That was the first time because this is now 1944. Stalingrad had fallen. Leningrad, had been-- the siege had been broken. They were already rolling down, down, down, I mean, from east to west. And the trains were full of wounded soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you thinking on that train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to get to home to be with my family, to be with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your expectation about the future at that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't, couldn't contemplate the future. It was just moment to moment. I should be home. I should be with my family. By that time, both my brothers had been conscripted in what was called the Auxiliary Hungarian Army. It wasn't really a Hungarian Army. It was a slave labor brigade into which the Jewish boys were conscripted. They wore a yellow band. They had no uniform, no weapons. And their job was to go-- they were sent with the Hungarian army to help the Germans on the Russian front. And their job was tantamount to being minesweepers because they were sent ahead to clear the fields and so forth. My older brother Moshe, who by that time was already married, and his little boy was born while he was in Russia, eventually, eventually came back in December 1943 with terrible news. I am backtracking, but we are talking about-- there has been so much. And so much was going on around that time that things do get a bit compressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- he came back from the Russian front in December 1943 with terrible stories about-- how should I put it? How did he put it?-- about bad houses where Jewish girls are taken to service the Nazis. He was talking about brothels set up by the Nazis. Because this is 1943, and we still didn't know what was going on. And he was very upset. And he told my father and mother. And the question came, what do we do with the young girls, which included me and two of my cousins? And I remember there was a very serious family discussion. And again, my father said, how can one believe such horrible things? After all, enemies are enemies but human beings are human beings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we cannot let our child-- because the idea was to send me and my two cousins away with-- with-- we had a washer woman, a  peasant, a Romanian peasant washer woman, who came once a week to do the heavy washing-- there were no washing machines-- and that she would keep us there, God forbid, if something should happen. But again, people are people. They cannot do these things. And besides, the Russians are rolling from east to west. And the final thing was, we cannot let our child go. And so did my uncle, the father of my two cousins, say. So we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was one thing. My other brother, Alex, he was also in this Jewish labor brigade, but he somehow managed to stay in Hungary. His second child was born two days before Eichmann came to Hungary. And my brother never knew that second baby. Time-wise, what's the relationship between the bad stories about the bad houses and you're going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the school. When did you know, when did your parents know about those rumors?

speaker: interviewee
text: December '43. But I was already in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about the rumors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I knew about the rumors. And this was discussed and settled-- or not only settled, but we are not going to go ahead-- while I came home for Christmas vacation. And, I mean I-- I just had no conception of these things. I was 16, 17. I had a sheltered life. Nobody ever harmed me. The Hungarians wouldn't let me to go to school, which I found so foolish and silly. And I felt it's the worst for them because I am a good student so they don't have me. But the [HUNGARIAN] Gymnasium has me. And-- and I really loved that school. It was great.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was going to do whatever my parents would have decided. And I was glad that-- that my life was going to go on. And the war is going to be over. That was the whole thing. That's where you left us, on the train heading back.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I think we should stop the tape and start--
